title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep20 - Sketch to Image Workflow with SDXL or Flux!
description:
In this episode of the ComfyUI tutorial series, learn how to transform basic sketches into high-quality illustrations using SDXL and Flux models! This tutorial guides you through each step, from setting up essential nodes to refining your sketches and creating final images. This video covers workflows for different sketch types, adjustment techniques, and practical tips on customizing your output. Perfect for artists, designers, and anyone interested in AI-powered image generation.
Unlock exclusive perks by joining our channel:
https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCmMbwA-s3GZDKVzGZ-kPwaQ/join

Get the free workflows and instructions from discord
https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3

#comfyui #comfyuistablediffusion #sketchtoimage

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction to Sketch to Image Workflow
00:16 Nodes and How to Update ComfyUI
01:12 Sketch2Image Workflow Presentation
02:04 Sketch Preparation Workflow
05:09 Image Variation Workflow
06:12 Sketch to Image Workflow
13:15 Drawing from Scratch Using Canva
transcript: Welcome to episode 20 of our Comfy UI Tutorial series.  Today, I'm sharing my step-by-step process for turning simple sketches into polished illustrations  using flux or SDXL models. Watch as I transform a basic sketch into a fully detailed, finished image.  We need a node for one of the workflows, so go to Manager, then Custom Nodes, and Search for Canvas.  We need the Canvas tab custom node made by this author. After it's installed, you can click Restart,  but I will close it because I want to install some new updates. By the way, scroll down and  always check the date. At the moment of this recording, that date is a few days back for me,  so I will click Update All and then click Restart. I will use some control net in this video,  so make sure to check other episodes like episode 14 because I will not explain it again in each  episode. Now, after the update, Comfy UI opened in a new window. If I go to Manager and check  the date, it's a new date, so it installed a new version. Sometimes Manager fails to update,  so you will need to do it manually from the Update folder and run the Update Comfy UI Bat file.  I will add some workflows on Discord so you can download them for free from there,  one for SDXL and one for flux. Let's start with the flux one. Now, this is an all-in-one workflow.  It looks complicated, but we have already covered most of it in previous episodes. There are  four different workflows and you can toggle them on or off using the switch next to each one.  Make sure to keep only one active. You can use any of the switches since they are synchronized. See how  when I use it, you can enable or disable a workflow. It's like a light switch that turns the light  on or off. Look at the title of each workflow. The first one is Sketch Preparation and it has the  same name on the switch, making it easy to enable. The second workflow is Image Variation.  The third one is Sketch to Image and the fourth one is Draw Your Sketch to Image.  So, let's enable the first one, Sketch Preparation. This is useful for preparing your hand-drawn  sketches so they are in the right ratio and luminosity to get better results when using the workflows.  I'm loading this sketch of a bottle that I got from one of our Discord members named Uday.  As you can see, the contrast and luminosity aren't great so let's decide on a ratio that fits  the image better. If I choose Classic Portrait and Run it, I get this two, three ratio image.  If I choose Balance Portrait, you can see the crop is a little different.  If I choose Square, it crops from the top and bottom so that won't work. In this case,  there are some little details in the corner that I want to get rid of so if I choose a four,  five ratio, I can eliminate those and the image looks okay without that little detail in the corner.  The aspect ratio looks good and we can remember that ratio because we will need it in other workflows.  Now, let's see how we can improve the quality a little. We have this node that lets us make some  image adjustments. You have brightness, contrast, saturation and so on. If I leave saturation at one,  it has that warm color tint from the photo but I like to reduce it to zero so it becomes basically  black and white. Then I can increase the brightness and it already looks better.  You can keep adjusting the settings and click Q until you like the results. On the right,  you can move the mouse and see the before and after. If we look closely, it's still a little blurry  so we can increase sharpness or enhance the edges. If we compare now, before and after,  it looks much better. This workflow is also useful for those who don't use Photoshop or other apps  and need to prepare sketches for social media. We can play with contrast as well, just make sure  the image isn't overcooked. Right now, the contrast is too strong and we've started to lose some  parts from the top. I think it looks okay now. A big improvement compared to the original.  So, that's the final image that is saved. I chose the save node but you can select the preview  node if you don't want all those variations saved in the output folder. If you right-click now on  the image, you can copy and paste it into other workflows. Just select the load image node and use  control plus V to paste it inside. Now, let's try a different example. I will upload this blue pen  drawing of an org. You decide on the ratio you want. In this case, the artistic frame ratio  looks good but the slim vertical ratio crops a little too much. I will go with square  for this one. Then moving to adjustments, the previous settings are too strong for this sketch.  So, I will make some adjustments. You could set the queue to change so it runs the workflow  each time you make a change. I think this could work for the final version.  So, we have the final version. Now, what? Right-click on the image and choose copy image.  We can go to the next workflow, disable sketch preparation and enable image variation. This is  just an image to image workflow that you can use to make image variations. And in this case,  we can create sketch variations. So, select the load image node, then use the shortcut  control plus V to paste it. Then you add a prompt for that sketch describing how it should look.  For denoise, it depends on how similar or different you want the image to be compared to the  original so you can increase or decrease the denoise. Make sure the ratio matches your image ratio.  In this case, square fits well. If I run the workflow, I get a different variation of that sketch.  As you can see here, we can try again and we get a different sketch. It's quite cool that we can  do that to explore ideas without needing to redraw everything. If I increase the denoise value,  I give it even more freedom and as you can see, the result is more different compared to the original.  Let's say I am happy with the result. Now, I can copy the image and move to the next workflow.  I will disable image variation and enable sketch to image. Select the load image node and  paste the sketch there. Or you can just load it and select it from the output folder.  I will add a prompt describing how the final illustration should look, including color style and  other details that can help. Make sure the ratio matches the image. I use all these predefined  ratios because flux gets better results at certain image sizes, specifically multiples of 64.  This is a flux workflow that uses flux control net. So, make sure you select the right model.  If you get an error with mat1 or mat2, it means you didn't select the correct model.  Also, this workflow is slower because it uses the flux model and control net. So, you need a good  video card. Then here, you can adjust the control net strength and the end percent, but I found  these values work well for most things. One more step is the preprocessor. This will greatly  influence the look. So, I made a list of preprocessors you can try. You can start with canny, for example,  and run the workflow. And the result is this colored version of that sketch.  Depending on the preprocessor, some will keep the lines like in this case while others will  just create an illustration without lines. Let's try with a different image like this anime girl.  I am adding a prompt where I describe how it should look, including the color of the hair,  the teddy bear, and so on. As soon as I run it, the image goes through the preprocessor, which is  canny in our case. And it detects all those edges, giving us an image that the AI can understand.  That goes through apply control net, which decides on the strength and when to apply it.  In this case, after 30%, it stops that control net, allowing more freedom to add colors  and improve the illustration. Since this is a text image workflow, the denoise is set to one.  The result is this nice illustration that is colored, the better the quality of your sketch,  the better the final results will be. Look how cool it is colored. We got the shadings with  lights and everything we need. I prompted for a white background, but you can even add environments  in the prompt to make it exactly how you want it. Let's try a different preprocessor to see how it  affects the image like this line art preprocessor. You can just search for line and find a few options  in the list. I will select the line art preprocessor and run the workflow and the result is this.  You can see it's different. It has fewer lines, so you get a different style. That's why  it's important to try different preprocessors until you find a style that you like.  You can observe how the lines look in the preprocessed image. Now, let's try a different one like  this line art anime preprocessor. As soon as I run it, you can see how the preprocessed  image has changed. If something is wrong with the image, you can cancel the running workflow,  make changes and try a different one, so you can test a few quite quickly. Let's say I want to  use this manga to anime line art preprocessor. You see that when I run it, it didn't run so fast  because I didn't have it downloaded. As you can see, it's downloading that model from hugging face  and some models can be quite large, so you need to wait for it to download. Also, if you get an  error saying incomplete, make sure you have enabled long path in Windows so it can download the  model. After a minute, it went through and produced this nice preprocessed image and the final  result is this. So try different seeds and different preprocessors until you find something that  works for you and then you can use a fixed seed and play with prompts to improve the final image.  Sometimes the sketch is too detailed or has too many lines that you don't want. In that case,  you can use the depth anything preprocessor. When I run the workflow, I get this depth map,  which gives a kind of 3D vision of the subject. You get results where the main shapes are there,  but the tiny details can be different. This is a good preprocessor when you have a rough sketch.  Let's switch back to canny and try a different sketch, like this futuristic building sketch.  This can be great for architects to come up with different renders from a sketch.  To get some nice prompts, sometimes I just paste the image into chat GPT and ask for a stable  diffusion prompt for that image. Describing the style and details I want to have.  I got this long prompt that can work great with flux. You can also ask it to remove quotes so  it's easier to copy. Now I will paste the prompt into the workflow and run it. The result is nice,  but it kind of keeps all the lines in the sketch. I might want to have more freedom,  so I could reduce the control net strength a little and see how it affects the generated image.  It still has those lines, so I could reduce it more, but I can also try a different preprocessor  that doesn't take into account all those tiny lines. Now if I change to depth anything and run  the workflow, we get that 3D look depth map of it that doesn't have all those lines, but retains  the main form I want. The result is this 3D image. We can try running it again with a different  seed and I get a different image, but with the same forms and proportions. Let's try a different  sketch like this cartoon bunny. I will start with the cany preprocessor, then I'll add a prompt  to describe how I want the illustration to look. I will set the control net strength to 0.8.  If you sometimes get blurry images, you can increase the number of steps to 30,  but most of the time you can get away with 20, which is faster. And this is the result.  It kept the drawing lines and also added color along with a fantasy forest in the background.  If I try a different preprocessor like the manga to anime, I get this cartoon bunny without the lines.  As you can see, you can get pretty much anything you want just by adjusting the settings.  Now, if you want a variation of this bunny, you could copy and paste it into the image  variation workflow. Just make sure to disable the current workflow and enable the one you want to use.  I can copy the prompt and try it and I get a different version of it. It's a little blurry,  so I might increase the steps to 30 and try again.  You can also adjust the denoise values, so you can have a lot of fun playing around.  Let's go back to the sketch to image workflow and load this simple house drawing.  It can be a children's drawing or any sketch you want. I will add a prompt describing the colors  and style I want. When I run it, I get this colored version of that sketch.  If you decrease the strength of the control net, you give it even more freedom, allowing for a  different variation. This can be used to create greeting card illustrations from different drawings.  Now, let's go to the last workflow, which we use when you don't have a sketch and just want to  quickly sketch something to guide the AI. Make sure all other workflows are disabled except this one.  Describe an prompt what you want to create. In my case, a futuristic building. Instead of loading  an image, we have this node called Edit in another tab. From the Canvas tab node, we installed  at the beginning. You just click on Edit and a new interface will open in a new tab.  By default, this opens with two windows, image A and image B, and you can move them if you click  and draw from this tab. Do you see this green gradient dot? This is important. The image with that  dot on will go to your workflow. So if I draw an S shape here and go to the comfy UI tab,  you can see it appeared there. If I draw something in image B, I click on the green dot to enable it,  and now that appears in comfy UI. To delete an image, you click on that dot in the top left corner.  To add an image, click on the New Image button and specify the size you want.  You can zoom in and out using the mouse wheel. If I draw something and check in comfy UI,  you can see that it's not there because I didn't enable the image. So make sure that green dot is on  and now it appears here. You also have buttons for Undo. If you draw something you don't like,  I suggest using Undo, but if you need to erase something, the eraser also deletes the background,  so I will not use that. Instead, I will just paint with a bigger white brush, the color of the background,  and then you can switch back and draw again with the color you want. Let's create a new image and  delete the old one. I will do some random lines just to see how the AI interprets them.  Make sure you enable that image. Now, when I run the workflow, I get that image pre-processed and  I see a building that has incorporated those lines. If I try a different seed, I can get something  else. I can also decrease the strength of control net to give it even more freedom, so those lines  are not as visible and the result is quite nice. Let's go to edit again, and this time let's draw a  messy shape to see if it still gets something interesting from it. And I got this building. Obviously,  the better the sketch, the better the result will be, but it can create something from any shape.  Let's see if I try some depth, anything pre-processor to see what it can interpret. It saw some  forms, but it could be better if my sketch were improved, and the final image looks like this.  For those who use Photoshop, you can do all the cropping to get the exact ratio you want using  the crop tool. From here, you can change the ratio, and if it's in the wrong direction, you can  switch it from this option. You can try different ratios until you find one that fits well,  just like we did in the first workflow. You can also enter your custom numbers here,  or make the crop smaller while keeping the same ratio, so you have a lot of options for cropping in  Photoshop. You can also adjust the sketch. I like to add a black and white filter, then adjust  brightness, and contrast to make the sketch stand out even more. Maybe I'll add some levels to make  the whites wider and the blacks darker. You can keep adding more adjustments until you're happy with  the result. For sketching, I prefer Photoshop instead of canvas because it lets me use my waycom  tablet for drawing, plus I have access to more tools like symmetry, which can save me a lot of  time, especially when creating mandalas, stars, or flowers. For example, here I can draw a quick snowflake,  then I will flatten the image and save it, or make a selection and copy the image directly  and paste it into the sketch to image workflow. For the prompt, I might describe it as a macro  photo of a snowflake, and for the preprocessor, I'll go with depth anything. The depth map looks  interesting. The result is this beautiful snowflake.
